blocks.content: Instead of laughing at him, she laughed with him.
He glanced back at the canvas because looking at her directly felt like standing too close to sunlight.
For a second, she was not smiling.
Then the brightness returned so quickly he wondered if he had imagined the absence.
She lifted the purple dinosaur slightly.
A laugh escaped him before he could stop it.
Cassandra seemed pleased with that, like she had won some invisible game.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Cass! We're going to be late.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I say a lot of things.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I have to go before my friend reports me to myself.
Ethan nodded, unsure what to do with his hands, his face, or the fact that he did not want her to leave.
Cassandra took one step backward, then pointed lightly at the canvas.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: And maybe make the fountain a little less sad.
Ethan looked at the weak stream of water sputtering behind them.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Artists are allowed to be hopeful.
A sentence that sounded playful until it didn't.
Before Ethan could think of a response, she turned and jogged lightly toward Lena, Winston tucked under her arm.
This time, he did not pretend not to.
When she reached the archway, she glanced back once.
Then she disappeared into the street.
Ethan stood there with a brush in his hand and paint on his knuckle, feeling like the whole morning had tilted slightly off center.
He looked back at the canvas.
But the page, the canvas, the whole ordinary day no longer felt empty with it.
Ethan dipped his brush into the paint and began again.
This time, he added warmth to the light.
